title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Manic Episode
content: The patient’s current medications must be considered. For example, a second drug is recommended if the patient presents while the condition is already managed with lithium monotherapy. Also, antidepressants are usually tapered and discontinued in a manic phase. First-line monotherapy includes a mood stabilizer, such as lithium or valproate, or an antipsychotic, such as aripiprazole, asenapine, cariprazine, quetiapine, or risperidone.

title: Mania -- Treatment / Management
content: Mania in bipolar I disorder was first treated with medications such as lithium, valproic acid, and carbamazepine. The treatments were focused on mood stabilizers and anticonvulsants which had shown efficacy in mood stabilization. Today, the class of mood stabilizers subsumes more than just lithium and antiepileptics--it includes many second-generation neuroleptics as well. A large metanalysis of medications used in acute mania showed that atypical antipsychotics were more effective than mood stabilizers for this purpose but not necessarily for maintenance of bipolar disorder. Examples of effective medications are risperidone, olanzapine, and haloperidol. Lithium, quetiapine, and aripiprazole were comparatively effective. Valproic acid, carbamazepine, and ziprasidone were more efficacious than placebo but less so than their previously mentioned competitors. Gabapentin, lamotrigine, and topiramate showed no difference when compared to placebo for treating mania. Clozapine and electroconvulsive therapy have shown many benefits for treatment-resistant mania but are less commonly used. Lastly, psychoeducation and psychotherapy are powerful long-term tools for patients with bipolar disorder as well as for their families or caregivers.

title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: The FDA has approved several atypical antipsychotic agents. These medications are available in various formulations, including oral and long-acting intramuscular (IM) formulations. Specific formulations may differ depending on the indication. Please refer to individual product labeling for detailed information on formulations according to indications. Aripiprazole: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia, irritability associated with autistic disorder, treatment-resistant major depressive disorder, Tourette disorder [4] [5] Asenapine: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia [6] Asenapine transdermal: Schizophrenia [7] Cariprazine: Bipolar I disorder, major depressive disorder, schizophrenia [8] Clozapine: Schizophrenia (treatment-resistant), suicidal behavior in schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder [9] Iloperidone: Schizophrenia [10] Lurasidone: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia [11]

title: Fluphenazine -- Indications
content: Although frequently prescribed off-label for psychosis or psychotic symptoms related to the major neurocognitive disorder and related dementing illnesses, all antipsychotics (including fluphenazine) are not FDA-approved for these purposes, and clinicians should avoid their use in such cases. Fluphenazine is not FDA-approved for augmentation of mood stabilization or treating concomitant psychotic symptoms in patients with bipolar disorder. Fluphenazine has also been used off-label to manage chronic tic disorders and Huntington disease to control abnormal movements and chorea.

content: Although numerous clinical practice guidelines exist for the treatment and management of bipolar disorder, there is not enough consistency to generate a ‘meta-consensus’ model. title: Chlorpromazine -- Indications
content: The efficacy of chlorpromazine in bipolar disorder was mainly established to control the manic episode of bipolar illness, such as excessive energy, decreased need for sleep, increased excitability and impulsivity, and grandiose ideations. Chlorpromazine is the Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved treatment for persistent singultus, a medical problem where hiccupping lasts more than 48 hours. Regarding acute psychosis, studies have shown that chlorpromazine has been effective as a short-term treatment in controlling combativeness and aggressive behavior in children. [2] [3]

title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Maintenance Treatment
content: Most patients with bipolar disorder will require maintenance treatment for many years, possibly lifelong, to prevent recurrent episodes and restore their pre-illness functioning. The current recommendation is for continuous rather than intermittent treatment, with treatments that were effective during the acute phase often continued initially to prevent early relapse. Mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics alone or in combination are the mainstays of maintenance pharmacotherapy.

title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Manic Episode
content: Add another medication if symptoms are inadequately controlled, or the mania is very severe. Combination treatments include lithium or valproate with either aripiprazole, asenapine, olanzapine, quetiapine, or risperidone. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) may be considered as monotherapy or as part of combination therapy in patients whose mania is particularly severe or treatment-resistant and in women with severe mania who are pregnant. Valproate should not be used for women of childbearing potential due to the unacceptable risk to the fetus of teratogenesis and impaired intellectual development.

title: Lurasidone -- Indications
content: Lurasidone is a second-generation (atypical) antipsychotic. It was initially approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of adults with schizophrenia. In 2009 the manufacturer decided to pursue approval for the treatment of bipolar depression by establishing the lurasidone bipolar program (PREVAIL). The goal was to determine a safe and effective medication for bipolar depression. Based on the results of this program, lurasidone received FDA approval for the treatment of bipolar depression in June 2013; this was a significant innovation because lurasidone was the first drug approved both as a monotherapy and as an adjunctive to lithium or valproate. [1]

content: Consider cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) as an add-on to pharmacotherapy. title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2210
content: Mental health behavior encompasses the broad therapeutic category of antidepressants, which serves as a parent class for a variety of pharmacologic agents. Within this class, esketamine is identified as a specific antidepressant, reflecting its role as an NMDA‑receptor antagonist used for rapid‑acting treatment of depressive episodes. Additional compounds classified as antidepressants include sulpiride, clorgyline, moclobemide, nialamide, and rolipram, representing diverse mechanisms such as atypical antipsychotic activity, monoamine‑oxidase inhibition, and phosphodiesterase inhibition. Lithium is listed as a child of the antidepressant category, highlighting its frequent use in the management of mood disorders and its adjunctive role in depressive treatment despite being primarily a mood stabilizer. Lamotrigine is associated with the therapeutic class of antidepressants, underscoring its common use as an adjunctive agent for depressive symptoms in bipolar disorder. Together, these relationships illustrate the interconnected nature of antidepressant therapy, the variety of agents that fall under its umbrella, and the overlap with other mood‑stabilizing medications that contribute to comprehensive patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 468
content: Antidepressants are a therapeutic class within the broader domain of mental health behavior. Several specific agents are classified as antidepressants, including casopitant, esketamine, sulpirid, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, sertralin, and tofisopam. Lithium is considered a child concept of antidepressants, reflecting its close pharmacological relationship to mood‑modifying therapies. The drugs bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are also placed in the antidepressant therapeutic class. Both forms of bupropion are contraindicated in patients who have the withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that they should not be used in this clinical context. Conversely, tofisopam is noted as a medication that may be used to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential therapeutic role for managing this condition. Together, these relationships delineate the categorization of antidepressant agents, their interconnections with related compounds such as lithium, and important safety considerations regarding contraindications and possible treatment applications for withdrawal syndrome.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
content: Within mental health behavior, antidepressants represent a broad pharmacologic group that includes several agents and related compounds. Lithium is listed as a child of the antidepressant category, reflecting its use as an adjunct mood stabilizer in depressive disorders. Lamotrigine, although primarily an anticonvulsant, is associated with the therapeutic class of antidepressants in some classifications, indicating its role in mood stabilization. Casopitant and sulpiride are identified as types of antidepressants, although they are more commonly known as an NK1‑receptor antagonist and an atypical antipsychotic, respectively, highlighting the overlapping pharmacologic profiles among psychotropic drugs. Bupropion and its salt forms, bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride, are recognized as child agents of the antidepressant group and are assigned specific therapeutic classes. However, bupropion and its salt forms are contraindicated in patients with متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), underscoring the need for caution when prescribing these agents to individuals at risk of withdrawal. Additional compounds classified under the antidepressant umbrella include klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisopam, each representing distinct chemical entities with potential antidepressant activity. Notably, tofisopam is reported to may treat متلازمة الانسحاب, suggesting a therapeutic option for managing withdrawal symptoms, which contrasts with the contraindication of bupropion in the same condition. Overall, these relationships illustrate the complex taxonomy of antidepressant‑related drugs, the importance of recognizing contraindications such as withdrawal syndrome, and the potential for certain agents like tofisopam to be employed in its treatment.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, and trifluoperazine are all pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These drugs belong to the class of typical antipsychotics and exert their therapeutic effect primarily through antagonism of dopamine D₂ receptors, thereby reducing the positive symptoms of psychosis such as delusions and hallucinations. Chlorpromazine, the first widely used antipsychotic, and its related compounds levomepromazine maleate and prochlorperazine mesylate share similar mechanisms and are employed in both acute and chronic management of psychotic states. Benperidol and trifluoperazine, also dopamine antagonists, are utilized when additional potency or specific side‑effect profiles are desired. The inclusion of these agents in treatment regimens underscores the importance of selecting an appropriate antipsychotic based on efficacy, tolerability, and patient‑specific factors to achieve optimal control of psychotic symptoms and improve overall patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2727
content: Antipsychotics are a class of psychiatric medications used to manage psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, while ondansetron is a selective 5‑HT3 receptor antagonist that is employed primarily as an antiemetic to prevent nausea and vomiting associated with chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgery. These two agents belong to distinct therapeutic categories; antipsychotics act on dopamine and other neurotransmitter pathways to alleviate psychosis, whereas ondansetron blocks serotonin receptors in the gastrointestinal tract and central nervous system to control emesis. Consequently, antipsychotics are not a type of ondansetron, and each drug class serves different clinical purposes in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1164
content: Methotrimeprazin and its hydrochloride salt are indicated for the treatment of both اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder) and اضطراب القلق (anxiety disorder). Oxazepam is used to manage اضطراب القلق but is contraindicated in patients with اضطراب ذُهاني, reflecting the need to avoid benzodiazepine therapy in psychotic conditions. The antipsychotic agents benperidol, chlorpromazin, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, trifluoperazin, and trifluoperazine hydrochloride may treat اضطراب ذُهاني and are also listed as therapeutic options for اضطراب القلق, highlighting their dual utility in managing psychosis‑related anxiety symptoms. Conversely, the antimalarial drugs meflochin and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in both اضطراب ذُهاني and اضطراب القلق, indicating that they should be avoided in patients with either psychotic or anxiety disorders. Clinical observations associate اضطراب ذُهاني with تعاطي المارجوانا (marijuana use), suggesting that cannabis consumption may be a contributing factor or comorbid condition in the presentation of psychotic disorders. These relationships underscore the importance of selecting appropriate pharmacologic agents based on the specific psychiatric diagnosis and avoiding drugs that are contraindicated in the presence of psychosis or anxiety.

content: Antidepressants are positioned within the broader domain of mental health behavior, indicating that they are a key therapeutic class for managing behavioral and mood disorders. Lithium is noted as a child concept of antidepressants, reflecting its common use as an adjunctive mood‑stabilizing agent in depressive treatment regimens. Additionally, sulpirid (sulpiride) is referenced in a classification relationship that suggests an overlap between traditional antipsychotic agents and antidepressant therapy, highlighting that some drugs primarily used for psychosis may also possess antidepressant properties or be considered in the context of antidepressant treatment. title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
content: Antidepressants are a central pharmacologic group within the domain of mental health behavior, serving as a foundational category for the treatment of behavioral and mood disorders. This class encompasses a variety of specific agents, including esketamine, sulpiride, clorgyline, moclobemide, nialamide, and rolipram, each of which is classified as an antidepressant. Although lithium is listed as a child of antidepressants, it is more accurately described as a mood‑stabilizing agent that is frequently used in bipolar disorder, illustrating the close therapeutic relationship between antidepressant and mood‑stabilizer medications. Similarly, the therapeutic class “sodá sůl kyselí valproové” (the sodium salt of valproic acid, or valproate) is associated with antidepressants; valproate is primarily an anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer but shares pharmacologic overlap with antidepressant treatment strategies. content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, methotrimeprazine, methotrimeprazine hydrochloride, prochlorperazine mesylate, trifluoperazine and trifluoperazine hydrochloride are reported as therapeutic options for اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder) and may also be employed to treat اضطراب القلق (anxiety disorder). These agents, which belong to the typical antipsychotic and anti‑emetic classes, therefore have dual utility in managing both psychotic and anxiety symptoms. Oxazepam, a benzodiazepine, is indicated for اضطراب القلق but is contraindicated in patients with اضطراب ذُهاني, reflecting the need to avoid benzodiazepine use in psychotic conditions. The antimalarial agents mefloquine hydrochloride and meflochin are contraindicated in both اضطراب ذُهاني and اضطراب القلق, indicating they should not be prescribed to individuals with either disorder. Overall, most listed antipsychotic and anti‑emetic medications can address both psychotic and anxiety presentations, whereas oxazepam and the mefloquine derivatives have specific safety restrictions based on the underlying psychiatric diagnosis.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 466
content: Antipsychotics comprise a pharmacologic group that includes several phenothiazine‑derived agents such as methotrimeprazine, perfenazin, acepromazine, promazine, and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride. Phenothiazine is the core chemical class from which many of these drugs are derived, and it functions both as a subclass of antipsychotics and as a subclass of antiemetics. Consequently, phenothiazine derivatives such as promazine and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride are used to treat psychotic disorders through dopamine‑receptor antagonism, while the same structural family also provides antiemetic effects for conditions like vomiting alone. Dopamine antagonism is a key mechanism shared by these agents, linking the concept of “dopamine antagonist” with the antipsychotic class. Because of potential drug‑interaction concerns, antipsychotic medications are contraindicated when used together with the antibiotic lefamulin acetate. This integrated view highlights the overlapping therapeutic roles of phenothiazine‑based compounds in managing both psychiatric symptoms and nausea, as well as the importance of recognizing contraindications in patient care.
